coredata.dc:title: A dually spontaneous reduction and assembly strategy for hybrid capsules of graphene quantum dots with platinum–copper nanoparticles for enhanced oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lixia
Hu, Chuangang
Zhao, Yang
Hu, Yue
Zhao, Fei
Chen, Nan
Qu, Liangti
coredata.dc:description: The unique hybrid capsules of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with platinum (Pt)–copper (Cu) nanoparticles have been prepared by a dually spontaneous reduction and assembly process of oxidized graphene quantum dots (ox-GQDs) and Pt precursor by galvanic displacement reaction on Cu microspheres. Cu@PtCu core–shell microspheres were first prepared by reaction of Cu with platinum (IV), which subsequently induced the reduction and assembly of ox-GQDs on them at room temperature. After removal of the Cu cores, the resultant capsules of PtCu@GQDs were collected. Based on the unique hybrid structure, PtCu@GQDs capsules showed remarkably enhanced catalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) with a 2 times higher mass activity and a 40mV more positive onset potential than that of commercial Pt black, indicating the promise of the newly-prepared PtCu@GQDs capsules as catalysts for fuel cell applications.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of hybrid capsules of PtCu@GQDs
Oxygen reduction activity of PtCu@GQDs capsules

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication process of PtCu@GQD capsules. (a) Cu microspheres were used as the templates. (b) Cu@PtCu core–shell microspheres were first formed by spontaneous galvanic displacement of Cu microspheres with H2PtCl6. (c) PtCu capsules were formed after removal of Cu cores. (d) Cu@PtCu core–shell microspheres induced the reduction and assembly of ox-GQDs on them at room temperature and Cu@PtCu@GQDs were obtained. (e) PtCu@GQDs hybrid capsules were formed after removing Cu cores. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image of ox-GQDs. The inset is a HRTEM of ox-GQDs. (b) Size distributions of ox-GQDs derived from (a). (c) AFM image of ox-GQDs dispersed on a Si substrate. (d) Height profiles along the lines in (c). (e) Height distributions of ox-GQDs. Scale bars: (a), 20nm; inset of (a), 5nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XRD, (b) XPS, (c) High-resolution C1s XPS peaks and (d) Raman spectra of the as-prepared ox-GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–c) SEM images of (a) Cu microspheres, (b) Cu@PtCu core–shell microspheres and (c) PtCu capsules, respectively. (d) TEM of PtCu capsules. (e–g) HAADF-STEM image of PtCu capsules and the corresponding EDS mappings for Cu and Pt elements, respectively. The insets of (a–c) are the corresponding magnified SEM images. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a, b) SEM images of Cu@PtCu@GQDs microspheres with different magnification. (c, d) SEM images of PtCu@GQDs capsules with different magnification.

(a) TEM image of PtCu@GQDs capsules. (b) Magnified image of the area in the white box containing PtCu@GQDs capsules. (c) EDS of PtCu@GQDs capsules. (d) the corresponding element contents. (e–h) HAADF-STEM image of a single PtCu@GQDs capsule and the corresponding EDS mappings for C, Cu and Pt elements. Scale bars: (a), 0.2μm; (b), 20nm; (e–h), 500nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Raman spectra of the PtCu capsules and PtCu@GQDs capsules. (b) High-resolution C1s XPS peak of the PtCu@GQDs capsules. (c) XRD patterns of the PtCu capsules and PtCu@GQDs capsules in comparison with standard diffraction peaks of Pt, Cu and PtCu alloy. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Catalytic activity towards electrochemical reduction of oxygen in KOH aqueous solution at 25°C. (a) CV of PtCu@GQDs capsules, PtCu capsules, Pt black in O2 saturated 0.1M KOH solution at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. (b) LSVs of PtCu@GQDs capsules, PtCu capsules, Pt black with a RDE at 1600rpm and scan rate of 10mVs−1. (c) LSVs obtained for PtCu@GQDs capsules at various speeds. (d) Koutecky–Levich plots for PtCu@GQDs capsules obtained from LSVs in (c) at different potentials. (e) RRDE voltammograms for oxygen reduction in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH for the PtCu@GQDs capsules electrode at 1600rpm. (f) Current-time chronoamperometric response of PtCu@GQDs capsules, PtCu capsules, Pt black in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution. The rotating speed is 800rpm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: A dually spontaneous reduction and assembly strategy for hybrid capsules of graphene quantum dots with platinum–copper nanoparticles for enhanced oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cluster Science, Ministry of Education of China, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China
Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory for Chemical Power Source and Green Catalysis, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory for Chemical Power Source and Green Catalysis

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Cluster Science, Ministry of Education of China, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China. Fax: +86 10 68918608.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unique hybrid capsules of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with platinum (Pt)–copper (Cu) nanoparticles have been prepared by a dually spontaneous reduction and assembly process of oxidized graphene quantum dots (ox-GQDs) and Pt precursor by galvanic displacement reaction on Cu microspheres. Cu@PtCu core–shell microspheres were first prepared by reaction of Cu with platinum (IV), which subsequently induced the reduction and assembly of ox-GQDs on them at room temperature. After removal of the Cu cores, the resultant capsules of PtCu@GQDs were collected. Based on the unique hybrid structure, PtCu@GQDs capsules showed remarkably enhanced catalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) with a 2 times higher mass activity and a 40mV more positive onset potential than that of commercial Pt black, indicating the promise of the newly-prepared PtCu@GQDs capsules as catalysts for fuel cell applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were single- or few-layer graphenes with a tiny size of only several nanometers. Due to the remarkable quantum-confinement and edge effects, GQDs possess the extraordinary optoelectronic properties and their application in solar cells, fuel cells and others have already emerged [1,2]. Up to now, various methods have been established to synthesize GQDs, including hydro-/solvothermal route [3], conversion of C60 molecules [4], conventional organic synthesis [5], chemical exfoliation of carbon fibers [6], electron beam lithography [7] and the scalable preparation of functional GQDs from graphite [8], as well as the electrochemical method we demonstrated [9,10], which provide the basis to support the wide application of GQDs.

To achieve the full potential of GQDs, GQDs-based hybrid composites and well-organized GQDs architectures have been fabricated, which could present the collective properties of the individual GQDs for many specific applications [1,11–18]. Apart from the optoelectronic devices of ZnO/GQDs [11,12], GQDs have been particularly used as catalysts or supports toward ORR. For examples, N-doped GODs were embedded on the surface of N-doped GQD sheets, which displayed excellent performance toward ORR in alkaline solutions [13]. GQDs were also served as the supporting substrates of Pt nanoparticles, showing the enhanced catalytic activity in ORR [14]. The existence of the covalent interaction between the metal nanoparticles with the GQDs was also verified [15]. Assembly of GQDs in a geometrically well-defined fashion opens the pathway to control over the optical and electronic coupling between the individual GQD units. Liu and co-workers have reported a new application of GQDs as ideal electrode materials for supercapacitors by a simple electro-deposition approach [16]. In this regard, we have presented the well-organized assembly of GQDs into microsized spheres [17] and one dimensional (1D) nanotube arrays [18], which show the great potentials as functional materials and new metal-free platform for efficient SERS applications.

Noble metal Pt is widely used as the electrocatalyst for ORR in fuel cells, but its high cost largely limits the commercialization of fuel cell for automotive applications [19]. As a consequence, Pt-based hybrids have attracted extensive attention due to the lowered cost and improved ORR activity [14,20–23]. In this work, we prepared GQDs rich with carbonyl and carboxylic groups on the surfaces, which were called ox-GQDs. The ox-GQDs were subsequently combined with the PtCu alloy nanoparticles to build the new type of PtCu@GQDs capsules. Remarkably, a dually spontaneous reduction and assembly process was rationally designed and utilized for the preparation of the final samples. Firstly, Cu@PtCu microspheres were obtained by the galvanic displacement reaction between Cu microspheres and platinum (IV) [22]. Secondly, GQDs were decorated onto the Cu@PtCu microspheres through a substrate-assisted reduction and assembly (SARA) of ox-GQDs on Cu@PtCu microspheres at room temperature [24], followed by removal of the Cu cores for the resultant PtCu@GQDs capsules. This unique structure showed largely enhanced catalytic activity for ORR with a 2 times higher mass activity and a 40mV more positive onset potential than that of commercial Pt black catalyst.

Cu microspheres (about 0.5–1.5μm in diameter, Alfa), chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6·2H2O, 98%, Sigma–Aldrich) and commercial platinum black (Pt black, Sigma–Aldrich) catalyst were used as received. For O2 reduction measurement, highly purified O2 (99.999%) was used. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

1.0g Graphite was added to 100mL concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) under stirring at room temperature, then 43.0g sodium nitrate (NaNO3) was added and the mixture was cooled to 0°C. Under vigorous agitation, 3.0g potassium permanganate (KMnO4) was added slowly and the reaction mixture temperature was kept lower than 20°C. The above system was then transferred to a 120°C oil bath and stirred for 12h. After that, it was cooled to room temperature and diluted with 500mL deionized water. The pH value of the solution was adjusted with sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) to 3. The ox-GQDs were collected after the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (cut-off molecular weight 1000Da) for 3days.

160mg Cu microspheres and 25mL H2PtCl6 aqueous solution (5mM, containing 5mM copper chloride (CuCl2)) were added to the 50mL centrifugal tube and the mixture was swung vigorously with hands for about 1min. Cu@PtCu core–shell microspheres were collected by centrifugation and PtCu capsules was obtained by leaching out of unreacted Cu core with 7M hydrogen nitrate (HNO3). The final product was washed with deionized water for several times and drying in a vacuum oven at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hybrid capsules of PtCu@GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of PtCu@GQDs capsules, the as-prepared Cu@PtCu core–shell microspheres were firstly washed with 1M hydrochloric acid (HCl) and deionized water, which were then added into the 10mL ox-GQDs (1mgmL−1, pH=1) and stayed at room temperature for 24h. The collected Cu@PtCu@GQDs were immersed into 7M HNO3 to remove the Cu cores. The final PtCu@GQDs capsules were obtained after thoroughly washing and drying in a vacuum oven at room temperature.

The morphology of the samples was characterized by using JEM-2010 high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) images and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) of the samples were determined on a JSM-7001F SEM unit. The elemental mappings were carried out on a Field-emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM) with high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) detector (Tecnai G2 F20 U-TWIN) operating at 200kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Netherlands 1710 diffractometer with a Cu Ka irradiation source (λ =1.54Å). Raman spectra were recorded using a RM 2000 Microscopic Confocal Raman Spectrometer (Renishaw PLC, England) with an Ar laser at a wavelength of 514.5nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with an ESCALab220i-XL electron spectrometer from VG Scientific using 300W Al Ka radiation. The base pressure was about 3×10−9 mbar. The binding energies were referenced to the C1s line at 284.8eV from adventitious carbon. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were taken using a Veeco D3100 atomic force microscope. The compositions of the samples were evaluated with inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) from Perkin Elmer instrument using flow injection online preconcentration.

The electrochemical measurements were performed in a three electrode cell by using a CHI760D electrochemical workstation. A glass carbon rotating disk electrode (RDE) loaded with the catalyst was used as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) (KCl, 4.2M) as the reference electrode, and a Pt wire (0.5mm in diameter) as the counter electrode. The electrolyte was 0.1M potassium hydroxide solution. The glassy carbon disk electrode was rinsed with double distilled water and dried at room temperature before the catalyst was loaded to the disk. The working electrode was prepared as follows. Firstly, 5mg PtCu@GQDs capsules was added into 5mL of ethanol and 0.5mL of 5wt% peruorosulfonic acid (PFSA), which is dispersed by ultrasonication for approximately 5min to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Next, 10μL of the dispersion was uniformly dropped onto a freshly polished glassy carbon electrode (5.6mm in diameter) and was dried under ambient conditions. By using the same electrode configuration, PtCu capsules and commercial Pt black catalyst with the same amount were also studied for comparison.

The ORR measurements were performed in O2 saturated 0.1M KOH electrolyte. Cyclic voltammograms curves (CVs) were recorded by applying a linear potential scan between −0.8V and 0.2V at a sweep rate of 50 and 100mVs−1, respectively. The measurement curves were recorded when the cycling was repeated until the reproducible CV curves were obtained. Measurements on rotating ring–disk electrode (RRDE, ca. 0.25cm2 for disk with a diameter 5.6mm and ca. 0.19cm2 for ring) and/or RDE (5mm in diameter) were carried out on a CHI 760D workstation equipped with MSRX electrode rotator (Pine Instrument) at a scan rate of 10mVs−1. RDE measurements were conducted at different rotating speeds from 400 to 1600rpm, and RRDE measurements were carried out at 1600rpm.

For the ORR at a RDE, the Koutecky–Levich equation can be described as follows [10]:
(1)

(2)

)

Here n is the number of electrons transferred per oxygen molecule, F is the Faraday constant (96,485Cmol−1), A the geometric surface area of the electrode, v is the kinetic viscosity for KOH (0.01cm2 s−1), CO2 is the concentration of O2 (1.2×10−3 molL−1) and DO2 is the diffusion coefficient of O2 in 0.1M KOH (1.9105cms−1). The constant 0.2 is adopted when the rotating speed is in rpm.

The electron transfer number (n) was also determined from RRDE measurements on the basis of the disk current (JD ) and ring current (JR ) by the following Eq. (3):
(3)
n = 4 × (
NJ

) / N
where N is current collection efficiency of the Pt ring, 0.37.The peroxide percentage (%HO2 −) was calculated based on the following Eq. (4):
(4)

)
Long term durability tests of PtCu@GQDs hybrid capsules, PtCu capsules and commercial Pt black were conducted through chronoamperometric measurements at 0.3V for 5000min with continuous O2 flow in 0.1M KOH. All electrochemical experiments were performed at 25±0.1°C. The Pt loading for PtCu@GQDs capsules, PtCu capsules and Pt black catalysts on a RDE were 0.0286, 0.0309, and 0.0395mgcm−2, determined by ICP-MS, respectively. In CV, LSV and stability curves, current densities were normalized to the same level. Current densities were normalized in reference to the geometric area of a RDE (0.196cm2).

The preparation process of PtCu@GQD capsules was shown in Fig. 1 . Briefly, Cu@PtCu core–shell microspheres were first prepared by spontaneous galvanic displacement of Cu with platinum (IV) [25]. The Cu@PtCu microspheres could subsequently induce the reduction and assembly of ox-GQDs on them at room temperature. After removal of the Cu cores, the PtCu@GQDs capsules were formed.

Fig. 2 a shows the TEM image of the collected ox-GQDs, which are monodisperse with a fairly uniform diameter of ca. 2–5nm (Fig. 2a and b). The lattice parameter was measured to be 0.34nm, corresponding the (002) crystal plane of graphene (Insert in Fig. 2a). The corresponding AFM image (Fig. 2c) revealed a typical topographic height of 1–3nm with average height of 1.5nm (Fig. 2d and e), suggesting that most of ox-GQDs are composed of 3–8 graphene layers [10].

Fig. 3 a shows the typical XRD profile for the as-prepared ox-GQDs. The ox-GQDs have a broad and weak (002) peak centered at around 25°, which is probably due to the existence of oxygen-related groups on the surface of ox-GQDs as confirmed by XPS (Fig. 3b and c) and Raman investigations (Fig. 3d). XPS shows a predominant graphitic C 1s peak at ca. 284.8eV and an O 1s peak at ca. 532eV. The O/C atomic ratio for the ox-GQDs is ca. 0.6:1, much higher than those of the N-GQDs (ca. 0.3:1) [9] and normal GQDs [10], verifying the as-prepared ox-GQDs with more functional oxygen-containing groups.

A high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s peak confirmed the presence of CC/CC (284.8eV), COH (285.7eV), COC (286.9eV) and CO/COOH (288.4eV) [10], indicating the ox-GQDs were rich in carbonyl and carboxylic groups on the surfaces. Raman spectroscopy of the as-prepared ox-GQDs (Fig. 3d) exhibits the features of D band at around 1354cm−1 and G band at around 1598cm−1. Like the ox-GQDs synthesized hydrothermally with a much more intense D band [1], the ratio of the “disorder” D-band to the crystalline G-band (ID/IG ) for as-produced ox-GQDs in this work is around 1.0, indicating there are lots of active sites on the surfaces.

Cu microspheres (Fig. 4 a) were used as the initial templates. After covering the Cu microspheres with PtCu layers by a galvanic displacement reaction of Cu with platinum (IV), the Cu@PtCu core–shell microspheres have a similar morphology (Fig. 4b). PtCu capsules (Fig. 4c) were synthesized by acid treatment to remove the Cu cores (Fig. 4b) [22]. The obtained PtCu capsules were homogeneous with the size between 0.4 to 0.9μm (Fig. 4c and d). Fig. 4d shows the capsules are composed of nanoparticles with a size of less than 20nm. The internal porosity among these particles will dramatically increase the accessibility, which could promote the catalytical activity. HAADF-STEM EDS mapping images of Cu and Pt elements of PtCu capsules were conducted in Fig. 4e–g, which show both Pt and Cu are distributed well in the capsules. The atomic ratio of Pt:Cu in PtCu capsules determined by EDS is about 1.1:1.

Recently, we have developed a substrate-assisted reduction and assembly of graphene oxide (SARA-GO) method for spontaneously forming the assembled structures of reduced GO on arbitrary conductive substrates [24], which enables us to reduce and assemble the ox-GQDs along the active metal surfaces such as the Cu microspheres here. As we can see, after incubating Cu@PtCu microspheres in ox-GQDs aqueous solution (1mgmL−1, pH=1) for a while, GQDs were spontaneously deposited along the Cu@PtCu microspheres (Fig. 5 a and b). The obtained Cu@PtCu@GQDs had almost the same appearance as the Cu@PtCu. After removing the Cu cores by immersing the Cu@PtCu@GQDs in HNO3, the resultant PtCu@GQDs capsules were collected (Fig. 5c and d).

TEM image in Fig. 6 a confirms the hollow structure of PtCu@GQDs capsules. Fig. 6b shows that the capsule wall is composed of nanoparticles in despite of the difficulty in clearly distinguishing between PtCu and GQDs. EDS verified that the C peak appeared for PtCu@GQDs accompanied with Pt and Cu peaks (Fig. 6c). The element content analysis (Fig. 6d) revealed that the O content has been decreased dramatically with an O/C atomic ratio of ca. 0.2:1, indicating ox-GQDs have been largely reduced by the spontaneous deposition process. The atomic ratio of Pt:Cu in PtCu@GQDs capsules is 1.3:1 (Fig. 6d) The atomic ratio of Pt:Cu in PtCu@GQDs capsules is ca. 1.3:1 (Fig. 6d), which is similar to that of the initial PtCu capsules (1.1:1), indicating that the active Cu core played the main role in reducing the ox-GQDs. HAADF-STEM mapping images of C, Cu and Pt elements of PtCu@GQDs capsules conducted in Fig. 6e–h shows C, Pt and Cu elements are distributed well in the capsules.

To further confirm the formation of the PtCu@GQDs capsules, we carried out Raman spectroscopic measurements for PtCu and PtCu@GQDs capsules. As shown in Fig. 7 a, PtCu@GQDs capsules present two featured Raman D and G bands characteristic of carbon-based materials, while PtCu capsules have no any Raman feature, suggesting the existence of GQDs along the PtCu@GQDs capsules once again. Compared to the initial ox-GQDs (Fig. 3d), the ID /IG ratio for the PtCu@GQDs has decreased (Fig. 7a), indicating the reduction of ox-GQDs. On the other hand, C1s XPS peak (Fig. 7b) reveals that the oxygen-containing groups have been removed significantly and the CC bonds become dominant for the PtCu@GQDs capsules, compared with that of the ox-GQDs (Fig. 3c), indicating the thorough reduction of ox-GQDs by the spontaneous assembly along Cu@PtCu microspheres.

XRD shows that a series of typical peaks in agreement with those of a standard PtCu alloy (refer to JCPDS ICDD card NO. 48-1549) are observed for PtCu capsules. All the peaks of PtCu capsules shifted to the higher angles, compared to pure Pt (refer to JCPDS ICDD card NO. 65-2868), because the lattice contracts when Cu atoms are incorporated into the Pt lattice to form a PtCu alloy (Fig. 7c) [22,26]. No single-component peak from either Pt or Cu was detected within PtCu capsules, thus confirming the presence of only single-phase PtCu alloy in PtCu capsules. Compared to pure PtCu capsules, no obvious peak shifts ((111), (200), (220) and (311) crystalline planes, Fig. 7c) shows the alloy feature of PtCu in the PtCu@GQDs capsules is the same as that of the PtCu capsules, which indicates the hybrid process had no effect on the feature of PtCu. The alloy feature of PtCu nanoparticles was further confirmed by XPS (Fig. S1). Additionally, the PtCu@GQDs capsules displayed a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ ≈25° assigned to the graphitic (002) planes. Such diffraction is the feature of GQDs in PtCu@GQDs capsules, consistent with the above results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxygen reduction activity of PtCu@GQDs capsules
ce:section.ce:para: Although ox-GQDs have been reduced during the production of PtCu@GQDs capsules, there are still considerable defects on the surface of the GQDs as verified by the relatively high D band in Raman spectrum (Fig. 7a). Such defective sites may enhance the interaction of Pt with O2 by relatively weakening O2 adsorption energy [27,28], and accordingly assist Pt to catalyst the ORR [13]. We benchmarked the electrocatalytic properties of the PtCu@GQDs capsules toward the ORR against both PtCu capsules and Pt black. Pt black is a kind of pure Pt nanoparticles with the size of ca.10nm (Fig. S2) and is used as the reference [19,29]. Fig. 8 a confirms the oxygen reduction activity of these catalysts in the O2 saturated 0.1M KOH solution (Fig. S3). For the Pt black, the onset potential of ORR is at 0.07V with a single cathodic reduction peak at around −0.20V. For the sample of PtCu capsules, although its reduction peak potential (−0.25V) and onset potential (−0.03V) of ORR were lower than those of the Pt black, its peak current density showed a remarkable increase. In contrast, the onset potential of PtCu@GQDs capsules is still at 0.07V similar to that of Pt black, but its reduction potential shows even a more positive shift (−0.18V) with a more pronounced increase in the current density. These results clearly indicate a significant enhancement in the ORR electrocatalytic activity for the PtCu@GQDs hybrid capsules. Additionally, the CV curves for all of the three catalysts were also tested at a scan rate of 100mVs−1 for comparison (Fig. S4a). The increased scan rate almost did not affect the onset potential (0.07V) and reduction peak (−0.19V) of PtCu@GQDs capsules, but contribute to the remarkable increase of the peak current density from 0.77mAcm−2 at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 to 1.13mAcm−2 at that of 100mVs−1 (increase of 50%).

To further investigate the ORR performance, polarization curves for the ORR on these three catalysts are shown in Fig. 8b. As can be seen, the ORR onset potential at the Pt black was around 0V, followed by a continuous increase in the current density. Although the ORR at the PtCu capsules commenced around −0.07V, the limiting diffusion current at −0.4V became about 1.3 times stronger with a relatively wide plateau in respect to the Pt black electrode. Compared to both the PtCu capsules and Pt black electrodes, the strongest limiting diffusion current (2.2 times stronger than Pt black) with a very wide current plateau was observed for the PtCu@GQDs capsules most probably due to the more efficient four-electron pathway [30]. Meanwhile, the ORR onset potential at PtCu@GQDs capsules shifted positively to 0.04V, indicating the ORR process occurred much more easily on PtCu@GQDs capsules. The enhanced activity of PtCu@GQDs capsules suggested the GQDs can to some extent promote the electrocatalytic activity of Pt components. This enhanced performance of the PtCu@GQDs capsules may be ascribed to the GQD layers that wrapped around the PtCu nanoparticles forming an intimate functional composite [13].

Linear-sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of the ORR for the three catalysts were also carried out to gain further insight on the ORR performance of the PtCu capsules before and after hybrid with GQDs. Fig. 8c shows LSV curves at different rotation rates for PtCu@GQDs capsules catalyst, and the LSV curves for the PtCu capsules and Pt black are indicated in Fig. S4b and d. As can be seen, the measured current density increases with increasing rotation rate due to the enhanced diffusion of electrolytes [10]. What is more, the limiting current densities obtained from the PtCu@GQDs capsules are higher than those at the Pt black and PtCu capsules at a constant rotation rate. The kinetic current (Jk ) and the transferred electron number (n) per O2 molecule involved in the ORR process can be determined using the Koutecky−Levich equation (Eq. (1)) [10]. The Koutecky−Levich plots (
J
D
- 1
vs ω −1/2) of the three catalysts are plotted for different potentials in the Fig. 8d and Fig. S4c and e. The parallel and straight fitting lines imply a first-order reaction with respect to dissolved oxygen [10]. The J k could be calculated from the y-axis intercepts (Eq. (1)) and normalized to the loading amount of metal Pt in order to compare the mass activity of different catalysts. The mass activity within the potential range of −0.40V to −0.80V were described in Table S1. The PtCu@GQDs capsules exhibited a current of 0.43mA/μg at −0.60V, which was 2.2 and 1.3 times greater than that of the Pt black and PtCu capsules respectively, indicating the interaction of GQDs with PtCu capsules largely contributes the electrocatalytic activity of the PtCu@GQDs capsules. This is probably due to the abundant defective sites on the surface of GQDs, which not only serve as an anchoring point for Pt components [31], but also enhance the interaction of Pt with O2 by relatively weakening O2 adsorption energy [27,28].

The n is also calculated from the slope of the K–L plots to be about 4.0 for PtCu@GQDs capsules, suggesting a four-electron process for the ORR as the case for commercial Pt black catalyst [10]. Fig. 8e shows the disk and ring currents on a RRDE for PtCu@GQDs capsules at 1600rpm with the ring electrode polarized at 0.5V. The PtCu@GQDs capsules coated disk electrode shows a high limiting current density of the ORR while an extremely low Pt-ring current density corresponding to H2O2 oxidation is obtained. Both the mean n and the peroxide percentage (%HO2 −) of the ORR for PtCu@GQDs capsules can also be obtained from the RDE voltammograms. As shown in Fig. S4f, the n was between 3.6 and 3.9 (Eq. (3)), which is consistent with the result from Koutecky–Levich plots. The peroxide yield on PtCu@GQDs capsules is less than 20% within the potential range (Eq. (4)), indicating the four-electron process again.

In addition, The durability of the Pt black, PtCu capsules and PtCu@GQDs capsules electrodes for ORR was evaluated by a chronoamperometric method at −0.3V in O2 saturated 0.1M KOH at a rotation rate of 800rpm [32]. As can be seen in Fig. 8f, the current density loss on PtCu@GQDs capsules is much less than that on Pt black and PtCu capsules after continuous reaction for 5000s. The current densities of Pt black, PtCu capsules and PtCu@GQDs capsules at 5000s were 0.14, 0.21 and 0.38mAcm−2. The stability of PtCu@GQDs capsules was clearly greater than the stability of the other two catalysts (Figs. 8f and S5). The instability of Pt black electrode is generally due to the migration and agglomeration of Pt when it was exposed to the hostile electrochemical environments [33,34]. On the other hand, the instability of the Pt catalysts is also attributed to the increased adsorption of oxygenated species (e.g., OH−) decreases the number of active sites accessible to molecular oxygen [35]. In contrast, the alloyed PtCu could effectively prevent from the agglomeration process of Pt domains, and the stability PtCu was further enhanced when the hybrid structure of PtCu@GQDs was formed [14]. These results further demonstrate that the hybrid of GQDs with PtCu can significantly enhance not only the catalytic activity, but also the stability toward ORR.

In this work, a rationally designed strategy has been developed for spontaneous reduction and assembly of ox-GQDs onto Cu@PtCu core–shell microspheres surface to form the unique PtCu@GQDs capsules. The resultant samples perform remarkably enhanced catalytic activity in ORR better than both Pt black and PtCu capsules probably due to the interaction of GQDs with PtCu capsules. Although the mechanism for the promising enhancement of PtCu@GQDs capsules towards ORR needs further explored in the future, we believe the mild substrate- assisted reduction and assembly of ox-GQDs method could be expanded to many other systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is sponsored by National Basic Research Program of China (2011CB013000), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21325415, 21174019, 21301018, and 51161120361), Fok Ying Tong Education Foundation (No. 131043) and the 111 Project B07012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.03.021.
